_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Calhoun County, IA. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Rockwell City is the county seat. Records are intact. Drainage district records are exceptionally significant for this heavily engineered county.

previous_courthouses.location: Lake City (originally the county seat) — frame building
previous_courthouses.fate: County seat moved to Rockwell City. Records transferred.
Replaced by current building (1914). Records transferred intact.

record_losses.description: Calhoun County has no known major courthouse fire or record loss. The county seat moved from Lake City to Rockwell City, and records were transferred intact. Drainage district records are exceptionally voluminous for this county — Calhoun organized more drainage districts per square mile than most Iowa counties, and the records document property ownership, tile installation contracts, assessments, disputes, and maintenance across decades.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} settled in Calhoun County, the records at Rockwell City are intact. The drainage district records are an underused genealogical goldmine — they document land ownership, neighborhood relationships, and the financial investments that shaped every farm.
record_losses.source: Stiles, Edward H. History of Calhoun County, Iowa. Cedar Rapids: Torch Press, 1916.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous from county organization. Federal GLO patents document original entries.
deeds.gaps: Light activity before 1880 due to sparse settlement — the wet terrain discouraged early settlement.
deeds.repository: Calhoun County Recorder, Rockwell City; Iowa State Archives

wills_estates.repository: Calhoun County Clerk of Court; Iowa State Archives

marriages.repository: Calhoun County Recorder; Iowa State Archives

court_minutes.completeness: Good. Drainage district disputes are a significant subset of the court record.
court_minutes.repository: Calhoun County Courthouse; Iowa State Archives

vital_records.completeness: Iowa vital registration from 1880. Rural compliance incomplete until approximately 1900.
vital_records.repository: Iowa Department of Public Health; Calhoun County Recorder; Iowa State Archives

substitute_sources.description: Calhoun County organized numerous drainage districts — more than most Iowa counties — under Iowa drainage law. District records include petitions for establishment, engineering surveys, property owner rolls, assessment schedules, tile installation contracts, maintenance records, and dispute proceedings.
substitute_sources.repository: Calhoun County Auditor, Rockwell City; Calhoun County Courthouse drainage records
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Drainage district records document who owned land within each district, what they paid in assessments, and who contracted for tile installation. Assessment disputes reveal family relationships and financial conditions. The engineering surveys show the physical layout of the drainage system — connecting {ancestor_name}'s land to the larger infrastructure.
substitute_sources.source: Prince, Wetlands of the American Midwest. Iowa Drainage Law.

substitute_sources.description: Federal population schedules 1850–1940 and Iowa state censuses (1856, 1885, 1895, 1905, 1915, 1925). The 1885 state census captures the early settler population.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives; FamilySearch; Ancestry.com; Iowa State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Iowa state censuses fill the gaps. Calhoun County's population grew rapidly after 1880, making the 1885 and 1895 state censuses essential.
substitute_sources.source: Iowa State Historical Society. Iowa Census Records Guide. Des Moines: ISHS, 2005.

substitute_sources.description: German Lutheran and Methodist congregations maintained records that supplement civil vital records.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual congregations; FamilySearch microfilm; denominational archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Church records supplement civil records before 1880 vital registration compliance was complete.

narrative_hooks: The drainage district records at the Calhoun County courthouse are the hidden archive of the Corn Belt — they document every property owner, every tile contract, and every assessment in the county-scale engineering project that made the farms possible.
The county seat moved from Lake City to Rockwell City — if {ancestor_name}'s earliest records reference Lake City, they are still Calhoun County records, transferred to the current courthouse.
Calhoun County was settled late because it was wet — the records before 1880 are sparse not because they were lost but because few people were there to generate them.
Iowa's state censuses are essential for Calhoun County — the 1885 census captures the first generation of settlers, and the 1895 census fills the gap left by the destroyed 1890 federal enumeration.
source_references: Stiles, Edward H. History of Calhoun County, Iowa. Cedar Rapids: Torch Press, 1916.
Prince, Hugh. Wetlands of the American Midwest. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1997.
Iowa State Historical Society. Iowa Census Records Guide. Des Moines: ISHS, 2005.
